design_philosophy.core_loop: Accept shadowrun jobs from Mr. Johnsons → customize character build with Karma system → execute missions with hired squad → earn money and Karma for reinvestment
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Freedom of character progression (full customization via Karma spending) balanced against meaningful build consequences (cyberware reduces magic effectiveness, class choice matters)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Cyberpunk mercenary operating in open Seattle with agency over how to approach jobs—samurai gunslinger, Matrix hacker, or magical shaman
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Embrace asymmetrical class design and real-time tactical squad management; player can break the class fantasy by mixing builds, but certain paths (pure magic) require commitment; open job board structure prioritizes player agency over narrative linearity

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Negotiation stat influences job difficulty and pay; player selects which Mr. Johnson contracts to accept; squad composition and hiring decisions directly impact combat difficulty; character build specialization creates varying difficulty curves depending on path chosen

progression.upgrade_system: Karma system allowing full character customization: player earns Karma from completing runs, killing enemies, or advancing plot, then manually allocates to specific stats and skills. Cyberware implants provide combat bonuses but reduce magic effectiveness, forcing build trade-offs.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Initial area restriction followed by gradual access to all zones via taxi, visa requirements, or helicopter; contact network expands based on completed jobs; magic restricted to shamans unless chosen at start; higher-tier Mr. Johnsons and better equipment unlocked through reputation/completion
replayability_hooks: Three distinct starting classes with different stat/equipment baselines
Infinite build customization via Karma system creates replay incentive for different specialization paths
Random job offers from Mr. Johnsons vary by contractor
Shadowrunner recruiting and team composition decisions

world_building_techniques: Mr. Johnson system establishes corporate fixer culture and shadowrunning economy
Mission variety (gang raids, extractions, retrieval, Matrix runs, ghoul hunting) implies living world with diverse threats
Megacorp headquarters and LoneStar building locations ground cyberpunk setting
Contact network implies emergent relationships and faction alignments
Cyberware/magic system implications (cyberware reduces magic, shamans only source) hint at tech-magic dichotomy in world

faction_indicators: Samurai archetype suggests corpo-aligned or independence through combat
Decker (Matrix specialist) implies corporate espionage or hacker underground affiliation
Shaman indicates magical traditions separate from tech establishment
Mr. Johnson connections track player faction reputation with contractors
Contact network suggests potential alignment paths
danger_signals: Cyberware implant reliance creates physical dependency vulnerability
Magic casting causes self-damage (balancing mechanic implies magical overreach carries consequences)
Squad wipe consequences (increased rehire costs) punish reckless tactics
environmental_storytelling: Club and bar backrooms as job distribution hubs establish underworld infrastructure
Megacorp building access as mission locations implies corporate power concentration
LoneStar building presence suggests law enforcement faction
Zone-gating via visa/helicopter implies geopolitical divisions
Shop variety (guns, cyberware, spells, decks) indicates established illegal economies
